lines: Istra touches the scratched bedpost with two fingers, then wipes them on her skirt.
"They carved into bedposts in the children's row. Not theft. Instruction."
Istra keeps glancing at the lower hall.
"I mended fences and patched roof tin. The chapel never acted kind, but it knew our hands."
"Down here the dead have better walls than the living."

choices.label: Ask if the chapel was home

lines: "A knife in a bedpost is petty cruelty. Petty is why it lasts."
"People lower their voices for the grand horror and shrug at scratches. Scratches teach a house to bow."
"Hinges, shutters, the south roof if it stops pretending to be lace."
"I can fix wood and tin. I cannot fix a wall that learned theology, but I can hang a blanket over the worst of it."
"No. Cloth, two nails, and a hand that hangs it straight."
"Some mornings the hand matters. A child can eat breakfast under a blanket without staring at the blood mark behind it."
"The old wardens packed people into the stone and called it order."
"I have patched enough cracks to know a wall keeps secrets badly. This one mutters in its sleep."
"Every day. Then Runa swears at a sack of grain, and Nessa insults the cistern, and the children make a court case out of a ball."
"A poor place. Still ours enough to answer when we complain about it by name."

choices.label: Ask whether she called this home

lines: "Home is a roof that leaks in familiar places."
"This one leaked over my cot and nowhere else, which felt personal. I suppose I missed it."
